title: 2018 Physics-Engineering Anual Program Review Update (Part 1)
text:
College of the Siskiyous PHYSICS-ENGINEERING Annual Program Update
1. Describe annual progress / changes / events in the program.
a. Program full time instructor Michael Read has announced that he
is retiring at the end of the 2018/19 academic year. Instructor
Read has directed the program for the last 35 years. The college
will need to make decisions regarding the future direction of the
program.
b. The college located and hired part-time instructor Patrick
McDougall to teach PHYS 2115 and ASTR 1110 Fall 2018, and
ASTR 1110 and PHYS 1010 Spring 2019. Mr. McDougall is doing
a good job with the classes and has expressed an interest in
seeking a full time position in the future.
c. For the 2018/19 academic year the college committed to offering
PHYS 2115, and ENGR 2035. This is the first time that these
courses have been offered in several years because they are
traditionally low enrollment courses. PHYS 2115 was taught by
instructor McDougall, and ENGR 2035 was taught by instructor
Read.
d. PHYS 1020 (Weird Science) was offered (online) for the first time
Winter 2018 and is being offered again (online) Winter 2019.
Current enrollment for the Winter 2019 offering is 18 students.
2. Describe progress on assessing SLOs and PLOs for the program.
a. All course SLO’s are evaluated each term using graded homework,
labs, quizzes and exams. Student achievement of course SLO’s is
excellent with success rates better than 80%.
b. The college has changed the method of documentation of SLO
achievement from Curricunet to Elumen. Due to delays and issues
with the implementation of Elumen, there is a gap in the official
documentation of SLO achievement. During this gap, SLO’s have
been assessed, however the records are kept with the instructor.

-- 1 of 2 --

title: 2018 Physics-Engineering Anual Program Review Update (Part 2)
text:
c. Program learning outcomes are tied to course SLO’s.
3. Discuss any support or obstacles encountered by the program.
a. After a several year lapse, the administration supported the
offering of PHYS 2115, ENGR 2035, and ENGR 2017. This support
is greatly appreciated by program students and faculty.
b. An obstacle for the program is that physics and engineering
students must take calculus. If the calculus course has a low
success rate, that directly affects Physics-Engineering enrollments
for the following two years. Instructor Read has a concern that the
success rate in first semester calculus Fall 2018 may be low which
could adversely affect Physics-Engineering enrollments in the
immediate future. A general obstacle to the program is that
program enrollments are interdependent on robust and successful
math course offerings.
c. Another obstacle to the program is that there is recent history of
upper level program courses not being offered. As a result, the
word is out in the community and amongst the students that they
may not be able to count on all program courses being offered.
This is likely having an impact on program enrollments.
d. Another obstacle is that the college has gone thru frequent
administration changes (including a current change in VP of
instruction). Each administration has a different philosophy with
respect to support for lower enrolled, but essential program
courses. Consequently, there is a lack of continuity from year to
year.
4. Describe program budgetary needs or implications. (Submit a CQIP if
you are requesting a budget allocation that falls outside of regular
yearly budget allocations).
See attached CQIP
